body_txt: The Disposable Fiancé is a sub-type of the Romantic False Lead that is extremely popular in romantic comedy films. In order to make a Boy Meets Girl plot stretch two hours, there needs to be some kind of obstacle in the way of True Love. At the same time, that obstacle shouldn't be something that will continue to be a problem after Happily Ever After , i.e. no significant character flaw, traumatic association, or impossible relative. So our heroine starts off already engaged to (and usually in the middle of planning her wedding with) the man she's not actually going to marry. This saves the writers from having to explain just how Mr. Not-Quite-Right came into the bride's life and lets them get on with their real job of getting him out of it. While the heroine and the male lead will spend their screen time realizing that they're right for each other, the scenes he gets will be dedicated to showing how wrong he is for her, so that when she finally ditches him (at the altar!) to run off with Chris Evans , no one will shed a tear. Here are some common approaches:  Assuming the fiancé isn't just a plot device, they can provide a lesson about how rushing into things is bad, getting married for the sake of being married isn't a good idea, and/or that you should really know someone before you make a lifelong commitment to them. If the setting allows it, an Arranged Marriage is a common method to preserve audience sympathy toward the protagonist. While this trope is not Always Male , it's uncommon to see a woman in this role. However, the protagonist of a "Reborn as Villainess" Story is usually a young woman who's supposed to become the Disposable Fiancée, or has already been victimised by the trope. Sometimes both leads will have a disposable intended, and in rare cases we end up with a Pair the Spares solution. Compare Disposable Love Interest , contrast Disposable Woman . See also Asshole Victim , which similarly diverts audience sympathy for someone who gets hurt.
